~ 
322-5901 
322~1 
322-6444 
Ken Masterton 
Californians for Nuclear Safeguards 
944 Market Street, Suite 309 
San Francisco, CA 
94102 
Dear Mr. Masterton: 
October 7, 1981 
This is in response to your letter dated September 15, 1981, in which 
you requested advice regarding the filing requirements of the 
Californians for Nuclear Safeguards, a committee qualifying a state 
ballot measure. 
Your specific questions were: 
1. 
When must a committee file a campaign statement disclosing 
its receipts and expenditures in connection with signature 
gathering activities? 
2. 
At what point do receipts and expenditures for 
·exploratoryft purposes become reportable in connection with 
a qualification effort? 
In response to your first question, a committee which is formed to 
qualify a ballot measure is required to file a campaign statement 
ft ••. not later than 35 days after the deadline for filing petitions or 
the date of notification that the measure has either qualified or 
failed to qualify, whichever is earlier (Government Code Section 84200 
(d)). 
During the qualification period your committee is not required 
to file any other statements until after the filing date for the quali-
fication statement. 
In response to your second question, based on our telephone 
conversation, my understanding of the nature of the expenditures for 
exploratory purposes is as follows. 
The committee is sending out 
mailings to groups or individuals with similar interests to determine 
future committee action. 
The committee anticipates such action to be 
either communication with legislators or other state officials regard-
ing possible legislative proposals or changes, or an initiative quali-
fication effort. 
If the committee spends funds in connection with the first possibility, 
communicating with state officials, the committee and any individual 
receiving compensation for such activity may incur filing obligations 
under the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
(See enclosed copy of th~Political Reform Act and Commission 
Regulations 18239 and 18620.) 
If the committee raises or spends funds 
in connection with ~he second possibility, qualifying an initiative, 
the committee will incur a filing obligation under the campaign 
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. 


Ken Masterton 
October 7, 1981 
Page 2 
My advice would be to keep records of all receipts and expenditures by 
the committee for exploratory purposes since such receipts and 
expenditures may be reportable if the committee incurs a filing obliga-
tion pursuant to the lobbying or campaign disclosure provisions of the 
Political Reform Act. 
If I can be of any further assistance, please do not hesita~e to con-
tact me. 
Yours Very Truly, 
~~ 
Political Reform Consultant 
Enc. 


(CALIfORNIANS fOR NUCLEAR SAfEGUARDS 
944 Market Street. Suite 309. San Francisco. CA 94102. Phone (415) 543-8072 
September 15, 1981 
c:,'

Lynn Montgomery 
Fair Political Practices Committee 
1100 "K" Street

Dear Ms. Montgomery, 
-
Our organization is looking to your agency far clarification on 
two issues pertaining to state ballot initiatives and FPPC reporting 
requirments. 
1. According to our reading of the" 1981 Information Manual on Campaig~ 
Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act", which your agency 
prepared, expenditures and receipts pertaining to the signature phase 
of an initiative effort need not be reported til 35 days after the 
deadline for filing petitions or 35 days days after the date of not-
ification that the measure has qualified or failed to qualify, whichever 
is earlier (Sec. E). 
Is this a correct reading? 
Does an initiative 
committee need to file anything prior to this with your agency? 
2. What is the litmus test for reportable expenditures and expenses, 
that is, at what point 
do exploratory expenses and receipts shift 
to"an actual initiative effort requiring reporting? 
It would be extremely helpful if you could respond to these questions 
as soon as possible. 
Your assistance on the phone recently has also 
been appreciated. 
Sincerely, 
~ 
f}U/.Jffi/~~ 
Ken Masterton 
Co-Ordinator

sections.question: 1. When must a committee file a campaign statement disclosing its receipts and expenditures in connection with signature gathering activities? 2. At what point do receipts and expenditures for 'exploratory' purposes become reportable in connection with a qualification effort?
sections.conclusion: A committee formed to qualify a ballot measure must file a campaign statement not later than 35 days after the deadline for filing petitions or the date of notification that the measure has either qualified or failed to qualify, whichever is earlier (Government Code Section 84200(d)). No other statements are required during the qualification period until after the filing date for the qualification statement. Exploratory expenditures must be tracked as they may trigger filing obligations under either the lobbying disclosure or campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act depending on their purpose.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks when a ballot measure qualification committee must file campaign statements for signature gathering activities and when exploratory expenditures transition to reportable initiative-related expenses.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that ballot measure qualification committees must file campaign statements within 35 days after the petition filing deadline or qualification determination, whichever is earlier, and must maintain records of all exploratory expenditures as they may become reportable depending on whether they relate to lobbying or initiative qualification activities.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: FPPC response letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Incoming request letter also included in document. High confidence extraction based on well-structured sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. When must a committee file a campaign statement disclosing its receipts and expenditures in connection with signature gathering activities? 2. At what point do receipts and expenditures for 'exploratory' purposes become reportable in connection with a qualification effort?

CONCLUSION: A committee formed to qualify a ballot measure must file a campaign statement not later than 35 days after the deadline for filing petitions or the date of notification that the measure has either qualified or failed to qualify, whichever is earlier (Government Code Section 84200(d)). No other statements are required during the qualification period until after the filing date for the qualification statement. Exploratory expenditures must be tracked as they may trigger filing obligations under either the lobbying disclosure or campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act depending on their purpose.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to Californians for Nuclear Safeguards regarding campaign disclosure filing requirements for ballot measure qualification committees and the point at which exploratory expenditures become reportable.
